title: Full-Size Storage Captain's Bed
summary: A full-size captain's bed built from three separate cubby boxes — one end cubby, two side cubbies — joined by a center slat platform, unlike the joined bench-module design used elsewhere here.
description:
This captain's bed builds its storage from three genuinely separate cubby boxes — one across the end and two running along the sides — rather than three identical joined modules, and skips drawers entirely in favor of open shelving accessed from the top. Build the end cubby first, then the two side cubbies, each one its own square box before anything gets tied together.

**Have the lumber yard rip your 1/4" plywood into 15" strips before you leave the store** — the plan's own tip, since a single ripped sheet is easier to haul in a car and only needs one crosscut per back panel afterward rather than a full sheet's worth of ripping at home. Attach the back panels to each cubby with nails and glue, making sure to nail into the center partition as well as the outer sides.

Once all three cubbies have their backs on, trim goes on the side cubbies, and cleats attach to the backs of the side cubbies only — **the end cubby doesn't get cleats**, since the mattress platform's slats rest on the side cubby cleats and simply attach to the end cubby directly. **Slats are deliberately not glued down**, laid on top of the cleats and screwed only — this is explicit in the plan, since it keeps the whole bed easy to disassemble for moving, unlike a glued platform that would need to be broken apart.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, ripped to 15 in strips, full sheet
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood, ripped to 15 in strips, full sheet
cutList.note: Cut to fit each cubby's back opening.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: From the six 8 ft 1x12s (actual 3/4" x 11 1/4") cut six cubby sides at 15", three cubby dividers at 12 3/4", two end cubby top/bottoms at 54" and four side cubby top/bottoms at 62". Those parts need all six boards: put a 62" piece with two 15" sides on each of two boards, a 62" piece with one 15" side and one divider on each of two more, and the two 54" pieces on the last two along with the remaining divider. From the six 8 ft 1x2s cut six vertical trims at 12", two end top/bottom trims at 55 1/2" and four side top/bottom trims at 63 1/2". From the five 8 ft 1x3s cut two center support cleats at 63 1/2" and eleven center support slats at 30 1/2" — that is exactly five boards, with a cleat and a slat on each of the first two and three slats on each of the last three. Have the lumber yard rip the 1/4" plywood sheet into 15" strips before you leave, then cut the three cubby backs to length once each box is built. Square every cut and label the pieces.
materials: 1/4" plywood, ripped to 15 in strips, full sheet

steps.title: Build the end cubby
steps.body: The two 15" sides run the full height of the box and the 54" top and bottom fit between them, which makes the end cubby 55 1/2" long outside — the same 55 1/2" as the end trim that goes on later, which is how you know the assembly is right. Fasten through the outside faces of the sides into the ends of the top and bottom with 2" screws and glue. Then fit the divider midway along the box: it is listed at 12 3/4", but the actual opening between the top and bottom works out to 13 1/2" (15" less two 3/4" boards), so measure your own opening and cut the divider to it rather than to the list, or you will end up with a loose partition. Check both diagonals before the glue sets.

steps.title: Build the two side cubbies
steps.body: Build both side cubbies exactly the same way, with the 15" sides running full height and the 62" top and bottom between them, giving 63 1/2" outside — matching the 63 1/2" side trim. Build them one after the other on the same flat surface and compare them before moving on; these two boxes carry the slat platform between them, so a difference in height between them becomes a bed that rocks. Fit each divider to the opening you measure, same as on the end cubby. Check the diagonals on both boxes.

steps.title: Attach the backs
steps.body: The 15" plywood strips match the 15" height of every box, so all you need is one crosscut per back: 55 1/2" for the end cubby and 63 1/2" for each side cubby. Cut each one to the box you are about to put it on rather than to the list, then set it on, line up one corner, nail that edge with 1 1/4" finish nails and glue, and pull the opposite corner into line before nailing the rest. Nail into the center divider as well as the outer sides — the divider is what stops the middle of a long back from drumming and what makes the whole box stop racking. A back nailed on square is what locks each cubby permanently square.
materials: 1/4" plywood, ripped to 15 in strips, full sheet

steps.title: Add the trim
steps.body: The 1x2 trim covers the plywood edges and the screw heads on the front faces. Run the top and bottom trim first — 55 1/2" pieces on the end cubby and 63 1/2" pieces on the side cubbies, which is the full outside length of each box — then drop the six 12" vertical trims in between them. Those numbers close exactly: a 15" tall box less 1 1/2" of trim at the top and 1 1/2" at the bottom leaves 12", so a vertical trim that does not fit means the horizontal trim is not sitting flush. Put one vertical trim over each box's divider and one at each end of the side cubbies. Glue and 2" finish nails throughout.

steps.title: Attach the cleats
steps.body: The two 63 1/2" cleats run the full length of the side cubbies and go on the BACK face of each one — the face that will look inward toward the middle of the bed — with the end cubby getting no cleats at all. Fasten each with 2" screws and glue, driving into the sides, the divider and the top and bottom boards, not just into the plywood back. Set both cleats at the same height, measured from the top of each box rather than from the floor, and check them against each other with a straightedge across the two boxes: the slat platform rests on these two strips, so any difference between them is a permanent tilt in the mattress. The 2 1/2" cleat projects into the opening, which is what gives the slats something to land on.

steps.title: Lay the slats
steps.body: Stand the two side cubbies parallel with the end cubby across one end; the 55 1/2" end cubby less the two 11 1/4" side cubby depths leaves a 33" channel between them, and with a 2 1/2" cleat projecting from each side the 30 1/2" slats land about 1 1/4" onto each cleat. Lay all eleven slats across, spaced evenly along the length, and fasten each end down with 1 1/4" screws and NO glue — that is deliberate, and it is what lets the whole bed come apart for a move instead of having to be broken. Screw the end cubby to the ends of the slats as well, so the three boxes and the platform become one frame. Check that every slat has full bearing on both cleats before you screw it.

steps.title: Assemble the bed
steps.body: Bring the three boxes together in the room where the bed will live rather than assembling elsewhere and trying to carry it — the finished frame is about 55 1/2" wide and 74 3/4" long and will not go around a corner. Screw the side cubbies to the end cubby with 2" screws through the meeting faces, checking that the tops of all three boxes sit in one plane so the mattress does not sit on a step. The mattress lands on the slat platform in the middle and on the tops of the two side cubbies at the edges, so the platform surface and the cubby tops need to be flush. A full mattress is 54" x 75", so it will fit the width with room to spare and overhang the end by about a quarter inch.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Fill every screw and nail hole with wood filler, let it dry hard, and sand it back flush — on a piece with this much visible trim, filled holes are most of what separates it from shop furniture. Sand all the outside faces and round over the top edges of the cubbies, which are exactly where hands and shins land when someone sits on the bed. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, and get the inside faces of the cubbies too, since they are open storage and will be looked into. Let the finish cure fully before the mattress goes on.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Full-size captain's bed built from three separate cubby boxes
